exercises.title: Audio as an image, Whisper as an encoder-decoder
exercises.prompt: Whisper is described as 'a ViT that eats mel spectrograms and speaks back.' Explain the input representation (mel spectrogram) and why Whisper is an encoder-decoder rather than encoder-only or decoder-only.
exercises.hint: Mel spectrogram = frequency-over-time 'image'; conv stem + transformer encoder reads it. Decoder generates text tokens, cross-attending to the audio. It's a transduction task: audio → text.
exercises.solution:
**Input representation — the mel spectrogram.** Raw audio (16 kHz waveform) is converted into a **log-mel spectrogram**: a 2D array of **mel-frequency bands × time frames** — essentially an 'image' of how energy in perceptually-spaced frequency bands varies over time. A small **convolutional stem** processes it and the result is treated as a **sequence of time-frame tokens** fed to a transformer **encoder** (with positional encoding). This is exactly the ViT recipe applied to audio: 'audio is an image of frequency over time.'

**Why encoder-decoder:** speech recognition is a **transduction** task — a whole audio clip in, a text sequence out — the canonical input→output shape.
- **Encoder-only (BERT-like)** could *understand* the audio but can't *generate* a variable-length text transcript.
- **Decoder-only (GPT-like)** generates text but has no natural bidirectional encoding of the fixed audio input.
- **Encoder-decoder** fits perfectly: the **bidirectional encoder** builds a rich representation of the entire audio, and the **causal text decoder** generates the transcript token by token, using **cross-attention** to look back at the encoded audio at every step. Whisper's decoder also conditions on **special tokens** (language ID, task = transcribe vs translate, timestamps), so the *same* model does multilingual ASR, speech translation, and timestamped transcription just by changing the decoder prompt.

exercises.title: Whisper's three bets and weak supervision
exercises.prompt: Whisper's headline choice was 'train on everything': 680,000 hours of weakly-labeled, internet-scraped multilingual audio rather than clean academic corpora. Explain the tradeoff of weak supervision at scale, and the practical robustness it buys versus the previous wav2vec2/HuBERT approach.
exercises.hint: Weak labels are noisy but plentiful and diverse. Scale + diversity → zero-shot robustness across domains/accents/noise, no per-dataset fine-tuning. wav2vec2 = self-supervised features + fine-tune per task, domain-brittle.
exercises.solution:
**The previous approach (wav2vec 2.0 / HuBERT):** self-supervised *feature* pretraining on unlabeled audio, then **fine-tune a head on a specific labeled dataset** per task/domain. High quality on the target benchmark, but **domain-brittle** (degrades on accents, noise, recording conditions it didn't see) and multilingual coverage meant separate models/efforts per language family. Required clean, expensive labeled pipelines.

**Whisper's bet — weak supervision at massive scale:** 680k hours of **weakly-labeled** audio (transcripts of varying quality, auto-collected, across 97 languages), no phoneme labels, no clean academic corpus. Trained as straightforward supervised seq2seq (audio→text) on this huge, messy, diverse set.

**The tradeoff:**
- **Cost:** the labels are **noisy** (misaligned, imperfect transcripts, some non-speech). Any single example may be lower quality than a hand-curated corpus entry.
- **Benefit:** **scale and diversity overwhelm the noise.** The data spans countless accents, languages, microphones, background-noise conditions, domains (lectures, podcasts, calls). The model learns features robust to *all* of it.

**What that robustness buys in practice:**
- **Zero-shot generalization:** Whisper works **out of the box** on new domains/accents/noise **without per-dataset fine-tuning** — often beating fine-tuned specialist models on out-of-distribution audio, even if it isn't always #1 on the exact in-distribution benchmark a specialist was tuned for.
- **One multilingual model** handling 97 languages plus **speech translation** (X→English) and language ID, instead of a model zoo.
- **Operational simplicity:** download one checkpoint, transcribe real-world audio, done.
The lesson's framing: trade *label purity* for *scale and diversity*, and you get the robustness that actually matters for deployment — the same 'train on everything' philosophy that drives LLMs, applied to ASR.

exercises.title: Whisper's failure modes and the streaming problem
exercises.prompt: Whisper is offline, chunk-based (30-second windows) and trained to produce fluent transcripts. Explain two consequences: (1) why it hallucinates / repeats on silence or non-speech and how to mitigate it, and (2) why it isn't a true streaming model, and what real-time systems do instead (relate to the Phase 6 speech-agent lessons).
exercises.hint: It's a generative LM conditioned on audio → can confabulate fluent text when audio is uninformative; mitigate with VAD, no-speech threshold, compression-ratio/logprob checks. 30s offline windows ≠ low-latency streaming; real-time uses VAD + streaming ASR or full-duplex models (Moshi).
exercises.solution:
**(1) Why Whisper hallucinates / loops, and mitigations.** Whisper's decoder is a **generative language model conditioned on audio** — it's trained to emit *fluent, plausible transcripts*. When the audio is **silent, noisy, or non-speech** (no real linguistic content to ground on), the language-model prior takes over and it can **confabulate fluent text** or fall into **repetition loops** (re-emitting a phrase, or 'subscribe to my channel'-style artifacts learned from web data). It's the same failure family as LLM hallucination: high-fluency output with no factual grounding.

*Mitigations:*
- **VAD gating:** run **voice-activity detection** first and only transcribe segments that actually contain speech (don't feed it silence).
- **No-speech / silence threshold:** Whisper emits a `no_speech` probability per segment; drop segments above a threshold.
- **Confidence heuristics:** reject or retry segments with **low average token log-prob** or an abnormally high **compression ratio** (a sign of degenerate repetition).
- **Constrain decoding:** temperature-fallback with these checks, suppress known junk tokens, limit repetition.

**(2) Why it isn't streaming.** Whisper processes audio in **fixed ~30-second windows, offline**: it needs a chunk of audio before it encodes and decodes, and it produces the transcript for the whole window. That's fine for transcribing files but imposes **high latency** and a window-boundary problem — it's not designed to emit words *as you speak* with sub-second delay. It's batch ASR, not real-time conversation.

*What real-time systems do instead (ties to Phase 6):*
- **Pipelined low-latency ASR:** combine **VAD + streaming/online ASR** (models that emit partial hypotheses incrementally) + **end-pointing**; use the 'flush trick' (start STT/LLM on stable partials, commit on end-of-turn) to hit the ~300 ms 'feels alive' range (Phase 6, lessons 14). You can run Whisper in a pseudo-streaming way with overlapping chunks + local agreement, but it's a workaround, not native streaming.
- **Full-duplex speech-to-speech models (Moshi/Hibiki, Phase 6 · 15):** abandon the ASR→LLM→TTS pipeline entirely, modeling audio↔audio over codec tokens so the system **listens and speaks simultaneously** at ~200 ms latency. That's the architecture for true conversational, real-time speech — Whisper's strength is high-accuracy *offline* multilingual transcription, not live dialogue.
